title: 89.	Count number of integers ≤ N that are divisible by at least one of given primes (inclusion-exclusion).
description: 89.	Count number of integers ≤ N that are divisible by at least one of given primes (inclusion-exclusion).

examples.explanation:
N=10, primes=[2,3]
Initialize count=0
Check i=1: 1%2=1, 1%3=1 → not divisible
Check i=2: 2%2=0 → divisible, count=1
Check i=3: 3%2=1, 3%3=0 → divisible, count=2
Check i=4: 4%2=0 → divisible, count=3
Check i=5: 5%2=1, 5%3=2 → not divisible
Check i=6: 6%2=0 → divisible, count=4
Check i=7: 7%2=1, 7%3=1 → not divisible
Check i=8: 8%2=0 → divisible, count=5
Check i=9: 9%2=1, 9%3=0 → divisible, count=6
Check i=10: 10%2=0 → divisible, count=7
Final count=7

N=20, primes=[5]
Initialize count=0
Check i=1: 1%5=1 → not divisible
Check i=2: 2%5=2 → not divisible
Check i=3: 3%5=3 → not divisible
Check i=4: 4%5=4 → not divisible
Check i=5: 5%5=0 → divisible, count=1
Check i=6: 6%5=1 → not divisible
Check i=7: 7%5=2 → not divisible
Check i=8: 8%5=3 → not divisible
Check i=9: 9%5=4 → not divisible
Check i=10: 10%5=0 → divisible, count=2
Check i=11: 11%5=1 → not divisible
Check i=12: 12%5=2 → not divisible
Check i=13: 13%5=3 → not divisible
Check i=14: 14%5=4 → not divisible
Check i=15: 15%5=0 → divisible, count=3
Check i=16: 16%5=1 → not divisible
Check i=17: 17%5=2 → not divisible
Check i=18: 18%5=3 → not divisible
Check i=19: 19%5=4 → not divisible
Check i=20: 20%5=0 → divisible, count=4
Final count=4

approaches.name: Brute Force O(N * k)
approaches.summary: Iterate through all integers from 1 to N, and for each integer, check divisibility by each given prime. Use this for small N or when simplicity is prioritized over efficiency.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count integers ≤ N divisible by at least one prime using brute force
ladders.desc: Complete solution checking every integer from 1 to N against all given primes
ladders.explanation:
This is the complete implementation of the brute force approach to count how many integers from 1 to N are divisible by at least one of the given prime numbers. The intuition is straightforward: we need to examine every integer in the range [1, N] and test if it's divisible by any of the k primes. For each integer i, we iterate through all primes and check if i % prime == 0. If we find any prime that divides i, we increment our count and move to the next integer (no need to check remaining primes for that i). The algorithm works because we're performing an exhaustive search - we're literally checking every possible candidate. This guarantees correctness but at the cost of efficiency. The time complexity is O(N * k) since for each of N integers, we perform up to k divisibility checks (though we break early when we find a divisor). Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects to the key subroutine (L1) which is the divisibility checking logic. Common mistakes include: 1) Not handling the case when primes list is empty (should return 0), 2) Forgetting to break early once a divisor is found (wasting computation), 3) Off-by-one errors in the range (should include N), 4) Not considering that primes might not be sorted (doesn't matter for this approach). The real code pattern involves nested loops with early termination.

Algorithm:
1. Initialize count = 0
2. For i from 1 to N (inclusive):
   a. For each prime p in primes:
      b. If i % p == 0:
         c. count += 1
         d. break (exit inner loop)
3. Return count

Time: O(N * k) worst case, O(N) best case (if first prime always divides)
Space: O(1)

examples.trace:
N=10, primes=[2,3]
Initialize count=0
Check i=1: 1%2=1, 1%3=1 → not divisible
Check i=2: 2%2=0 → divisible, count=1
Check i=3: 3%2=1, 3%3=0 → divisible, count=2
Check i=4: 4%2=0 → divisible, count=3
Check i=5: 5%2=1, 5%3=2 → not divisible
Check i=6: 6%2=0 → divisible, count=4
Check i=7: 7%2=1, 7%3=1 → not divisible
Check i=8: 8%2=0 → divisible, count=5
Check i=9: 9%2=1, 9%3=0 → divisible, count=6
Check i=10: 10%2=0 → divisible, count=7
Final count=7

N=20, primes=[5]
Initialize count=0
Check i=1: 1%5=1 → not divisible
Check i=2: 2%5=2 → not divisible
Check i=3: 3%5=3 → not divisible
Check i=4: 4%5=4 → not divisible
Check i=5: 5%5=0 → divisible, count=1
Check i=6: 6%5=1 → not divisible
Check i=7: 7%5=2 → not divisible
Check i=8: 8%5=3 → not divisible
Check i=9: 9%5=4 → not divisible
Check i=10: 10%5=0 → divisible, count=2
Check i=11: 11%5=1 → not divisible
Check i=12: 12%5=2 → not divisible
Check i=13: 13%5=3 → not divisible
Check i=14: 14%5=4 → not divisible
Check i=15: 15%5=0 → divisible, count=3
Check i=16: 16%5=1 → not divisible
Check i=17: 17%5=2 → not divisible
Check i=18: 18%5=3 → not divisible
Check i=19: 19%5=4 → not divisible
Check i=20: 20%5=0 → divisible, count=4
Final count=4

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check if a number is divisible by any prime in a list
ladders.desc: Determine if a given integer is divisible by at least one prime from a list
ladders.explanation:
This is the core checking logic that forms the heart of the brute force solution. Given a single integer n and a list of primes, we need to determine whether n is divisible by at least one prime in the list. The intuition is simple: a number n is divisible by prime p if n modulo p equals zero (n % p == 0). We iterate through the primes list and return true as soon as we find any prime that divides n. If we exhaust all primes without finding a divisor, we return false. This early termination is crucial for efficiency - once we find one divisor, we don't need to check the remaining primes. The time complexity is O(k) where k is the number of primes, but with early termination it could be O(1) in best case. Space complexity is O(1). This subroutine connects to the core logic (L2) of iterating through all numbers. Common mistakes: 1) Not using early termination (checking all primes even after finding a divisor), 2) Forgetting to handle empty primes list (should return false), 3) Confusing divisibility with other number properties. The real code pattern is a simple loop with conditional check and early return.

Algorithm:
1. For each prime p in primes:
   a. If n % p == 0:
      b. Return true
2. Return false

Time: O(k) worst case, O(1) best case
Space: O(1)

examples.trace:
n=12, primes=[2,3,5]
Check p=2: 12%2=0 → divisible, return true
(Don't check remaining primes)
Result: true

n=7, primes=[2,3,5]
Check p=2: 7%2=1 → not divisible
Check p=3: 7%3=1 → not divisible
Check p=5: 7%5=2 → not divisible
All primes checked, none divide 7
Return false
Result: false

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterate through all integers from 1 to N
ladders.desc: Loop through every integer in the range and apply a condition
ladders.explanation:
This level focuses on the main iteration structure of the brute force approach. The core idea is to examine every integer from 1 to N (inclusive) and perform some operation on each. In the context of our problem, the operation is checking divisibility by primes, but this pattern appears in many brute force algorithms. The intuition is that to count how many numbers satisfy a condition, we can simply test every candidate. This guarantees we don't miss any solutions but becomes inefficient for large N. The algorithm uses a simple for-loop from 1 to N. For each i, we would typically call a helper function (like the L1 subroutine) to check the condition. The time complexity of just the iteration is O(N), and space is O(1) for the loop counter. This connects to the building block (L3) of range iteration. Common mistakes: 1) Off-by-one errors (starting at 0 instead of 1, or going to N-1 instead of N), 2) Using while loops incorrectly, 3) Not considering that N could be 0 or negative (though problem states N ≥ 1). The real code pattern is a simple for-loop with range.

Algorithm:
1. Initialize count = 0
2. For i from 1 to N (inclusive):
   a. If condition(i) is true:
      b. count += 1
3. Return count

Time: O(N)
Space: O(1)

examples.trace:
N=5, count even numbers
Initialize count=0
Check i=1: 1%2=1 → not even
Check i=2: 2%2=0 → even, count=1
Check i=3: 3%2=1 → not even
Check i=4: 4%2=0 → even, count=2
Check i=5: 5%2=1 → not even
Final count=2

N=8, count numbers divisible by 3
Initialize count=0
Check i=1: 1%3=1 → not divisible
Check i=2: 2%3=2 → not divisible
Check i=3: 3%3=0 → divisible, count=1
Check i=4: 4%3=1 → not divisible
Check i=5: 5%3=2 → not divisible
Check i=6: 6%3=0 → divisible, count=2
Check i=7: 7%3=1 → not divisible
Check i=8: 8%3=2 → not divisible
Final count=2

ladders.title: Building Block — Check divisibility of one number by another
ladders.desc: Determine if integer a is divisible by integer b
ladders.explanation:
This is a fundamental arithmetic operation that underpins the entire solution. We need to determine whether one integer (a) is divisible by another integer (b), which means b divides a without remainder. The mathematical definition: a is divisible by b if there exists an integer k such that a = b × k. In programming terms, this is equivalent to a % b == 0 (the modulo operation gives remainder 0). The intuition is that division has two results: quotient and remainder. When remainder is zero, the numbers divide evenly. This operation is O(1) time and space. This connects to the basic operation (L4) of modulo arithmetic. Common mistakes: 1) Confusing divisibility with other relations (like 'a is multiple of b' is same as 'b divides a'), 2) Not handling b=0 case (division by zero - though in our problem b is prime so never 0), 3) Using integer division instead of modulo. The real code pattern is a simple conditional with modulo operator.

Algorithm:
1. If b == 0: return false (or error)
2. Return (a % b == 0)

Time: O(1)
Space: O(1)

examples.trace:
a=10, b=2
Compute 10 % 2 = 0
0 == 0 is true
Result: true (10 is divisible by 2)

a=10, b=3
Compute 10 % 3 = 1
1 == 0 is false
Result: false (10 is not divisible by 3)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo (remainder) operation
ladders.desc: Compute the remainder when dividing two integers
ladders.explanation:
The modulo operation, written as a % b, gives the remainder when integer a is divided by integer b. This is one of the most fundamental operations in computer science and mathematics. Formally, a % b = a - b × floor(a/b). The intuition: when we divide a by b, we get a quotient (how many times b fits completely into a) and a remainder (what's left over). The modulo gives us that remainder. For example, 10 % 3 = 1 because 10 ÷ 3 = 3 with remainder 1. Key properties: 1) 0 ≤ (a % b) < |b|, 2) a % b = 0 when b divides a evenly, 3) (a + b) % m = ((a % m) + (b % m)) % m. This operation is O(1) time and space. This connects to the concept foundation (L5) of integer arithmetic. Common mistakes: 1) Confusing modulo with integer division, 2) Not understanding negative number behavior (implementation-defined in some languages), 3) Assuming a % b is always positive (it's not for negative a). The real code pattern is using the % operator.

Algorithm:
1. Return a - b * (a // b)  # integer division

Time: O(1)
Space: O(1)

examples.trace:
a=10, b=3
Compute integer division: 10 // 3 = 3
Compute 10 - 3 × 3 = 10 - 9 = 1
Result: 1

a=15, b=5
Compute integer division: 15 // 5 = 3
Compute 15 - 5 × 3 = 15 - 15 = 0
Result: 0

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Integer arithmetic operations
ladders.desc: Basic mathematical operations on integers: addition, subtraction, multiplication, division
ladders.explanation:
This is the absolute foundation - understanding how to perform basic arithmetic operations on integers. Integers are whole numbers (positive, negative, or zero) without fractional parts. The four basic operations are: 1) Addition (+): combining quantities, 2) Subtraction (-): finding difference, 3) Multiplication (×): repeated addition, 4) Division (÷): splitting into equal parts. For integers, division has two important variants: integer division (//) which gives the quotient, and modulo (%) which gives the remainder. For example, 10 ÷ 3 = 3 with remainder 1, so 10 // 3 = 3 and 10 % 3 = 1. The intuition is that these operations allow us to manipulate and reason about quantities. All operations are O(1) time and space. This connects to everything above - we need arithmetic to check divisibility. Common beginner mistakes: 1) Confusing integer division with float division, 2) Not understanding operator precedence, 3) Division by zero errors. The real code pattern uses +, -, *, //, % operators.

Algorithm examples:
1. Addition: a + b
2. Subtraction: a - b
3. Multiplication: a * b
4. Integer division: a // b
5. Modulo: a % b

Time: O(1) for all
Space: O(1)

examples.output: Addition: 10, Subtraction: 4, Multiplication: 21, Int division: 2, Modulo: 1
examples.trace:
a=7, b=3
Addition: 7 + 3 = 10
Subtraction: 7 - 3 = 4
Multiplication: 7 × 3 = 21
Integer division: 7 ÷ 3 = 2 remainder 1, so 7 // 3 = 2
Modulo: remainder is 1, so 7 % 3 = 1

examples.output: Addition: 12, Subtraction: 8, Multiplication: 20, Int division: 5, Modulo: 0
examples.trace:
a=10, b=2
Addition: 10 + 2 = 12
Subtraction: 10 - 2 = 8
Multiplication: 10 × 2 = 20
Integer division: 10 ÷ 2 = 5 exactly, so 10 // 2 = 5
Modulo: no remainder, so 10 % 2 = 0

testCases.expected: Addition: 7, Subtraction: 3, Multiplication: 10, Int division: 2, Modulo: 1
Addition: 12, Subtraction: 4, Multiplication: 32, Int division: 2, Modulo: 0
Addition: 17, Subtraction: 7, Multiplication: 60, Int division: 2, Modulo: 2
Addition: 18, Subtraction: 12, Multiplication: 45, Int division: 5, Modulo: 0
Addition: 26, Subtraction: 14, Multiplication: 120, Int division: 3, Modulo: 2
Addition: 32, Subtraction: 18, Multiplication: 175, Int division: 3, Modulo: 4
Addition: 38, Subtraction: 22, Multiplication: 240, Int division: 3, Modulo: 6
Addition: 109, Subtraction: 91, Multiplication: 900, Int division: 11, Modulo: 1
Addition: 110, Subtraction: 90, Multiplication: 1000, Int division: 10, Modulo: 0
Addition: 2, Subtraction: 0, Multiplication: 1, Int division: 1, Modulo: 0

approaches.name: Inclusion-Exclusion with Recursion O(2^k)
approaches.summary: Apply the inclusion-exclusion principle recursively to count numbers divisible by at least one prime. Generate all subsets of primes, compute LCMs, and adjust counts based on subset size parity. Suitable when k (number of primes) is small.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Numbers Divisible by At Least One Prime Using Inclusion-Exclusion
ladders.desc: Complete solution using recursive inclusion-exclusion to count numbers ≤ N divisible by at least one given prime.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting integers from 1 to N that are divisible by at least one prime from a given list. The inclusion-exclusion principle provides an elegant solution when the number of primes k is small (typically ≤ 20). The key insight is that counting numbers divisible by 'at least one' is challenging directly, but we can compute it by summing counts for individual primes, subtracting overlaps for pairs, adding back for triples, and so on. This alternating pattern emerges from the principle of inclusion-exclusion. We generate all 2^k subsets of primes (excluding the empty set), compute the LCM for each subset, count how many numbers ≤ N are divisible by that LCM, and add or subtract based on subset size parity (odd-sized subsets add, even-sized subtract). The recursive implementation explores all subsets systematically while computing LCMs incrementally. Time complexity is O(2^k) since we generate all subsets, and space is O(k) for recursion depth and storing primes. This approach works because each integer's contribution is counted exactly once due to the alternating signs. Common mistakes include forgetting to exclude the empty subset, integer overflow when computing LCMs, and incorrect handling of large N where division must use integer arithmetic. The connection to L1 is that this level integrates the recursive subset generation with LCM computation and sign application to solve the complete problem.

examples.trace: Step 1: Generate all non-empty subsets of [2,3]. Subsets: {2}, {3}, {2,3}. Step 2: For subset {2}: LCM=2, count=floor(20/2)=10, subset size=1 (odd), add 10. Step 3: For subset {3}: LCM=3, count=floor(20/3)=6, subset size=1 (odd), add 6. Step 4: For subset {2,3}: LCM=lcm(2,3)=6, count=floor(20/6)=3, subset size=2 (even), subtract 3. Step 5: Total = 10 + 6 - 3 = 13. Verification: Numbers divisible by 2 or 3 up to 20: 2,3,4,6,8,9,10,12,14,15,16,18,20 → 13 numbers.

Step 1: Generate all non-empty subsets of [2,3,5] (7 subsets). Step 2: Process subsets: {2}: LCM=2, count=15, add 15. {3}: LCM=3, count=10, add 10. {5}: LCM=5, count=6, add 6. {2,3}: LCM=6, count=5, subtract 5. {2,5}: LCM=10, count=3, subtract 3. {3,5}: LCM=15, count=2, subtract 2. {2,3,5}: LCM=30, count=1, add 1. Step 3: Total = 15+10+6-5-3-2+1 = 22. Verification: Numbers divisible by 2,3, or 5 up to 30: 2,3,4,5,6,8,9,10,12,14,15,16,18,20,21,22,24,25,26,27,28,30 → 22 numbers.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Subset Generation with LCM and Sign Application
ladders.desc: Generate all subsets of primes recursively, compute LCM for each, and apply inclusion-exclusion sign.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine forms the core of the inclusion-exclusion solution. The task is to traverse all 2^k subsets of the prime list (excluding empty set) using recursion, compute the LCM for the current subset incrementally, and accumulate the count with appropriate sign (+ for odd-sized subsets, - for even-sized). The intuition is that recursion naturally explores the subset space: at each step, we decide whether to include the current prime or not. When we include a prime, we update the LCM by computing lcm(current_lcm, prime), increment subset size, and continue. When the recursion reaches the end of the list, if the subset is non-empty, we compute count = N // lcm and add or subtract based on size parity. This avoids explicitly storing all subsets, saving space. Time complexity is O(2^k) as we explore all subsets, and space is O(k) for recursion stack. The connection to L0 is that this sub-routine is called once from the main function to compute the total count. Common mistakes include not handling the base case correctly (when index == len(primes)), forgetting to pass updated lcm and size parameters, and integer overflow in LCM computation. The recursive approach is preferred for clarity, though iterative bitmasking is an alternative.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at index 0, lcm=1, size=0. Step 2: Exclude prime 2: recurse with index=1, lcm=1, size=0. Step 3: At index 1, exclude prime 3: reach end, subset empty → return 0. Step 4: At index 1, include prime 3: lcm=lcm(1,3)=3, size=1, recurse to end, count=12//3=4, odd size → add 4, return 4. Step 5: Back to index 0, include prime 2: lcm=lcm(1,2)=2, size=1, recurse to index=1. Step 6: At index 1, exclude prime 3: reach end, count=12//2=6, odd size → add 6, return 6. Step 7: At index 1, include prime 3: lcm=lcm(2,3)=6, size=2, reach end, count=12//6=2, even size → subtract 2, return 2. Step 8: Total from include branch at index 0: 6 + (-2) = 4. Step 9: Final total: exclude branch (0) + include branch (4) = 4. But wait, this is partial; full total should be 8? Let's recompute: Actually, the recursion should sum all paths. Correct trace: Start: index=0, lcm=1, size=0. Path1: exclude 2, exclude 3 → empty, return 0. Path2: exclude 2, include 3 → lcm=3, size=1, count=4, return 4. Path3: include 2, exclude 3 → lcm=2, size=1, count=6, return 6. Path4: include 2, include 3 → lcm=6, size=2, count=2, return -2. Sum: 0+4+6-2=8. Yes, total 8.

Step 1: Start at index 0, lcm=1, size=0. Step 2: Exclude prime 2: recurse to index=1 (end), subset empty → return 0. Step 3: Include prime 2: lcm=lcm(1,2)=2, size=1, recurse to end, count=10//2=5, odd size → add 5, return 5. Step 4: Total: 0 + 5 = 5.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Inclusion-Exclusion Principle for 'At Least One' Condition
ladders.desc: Apply inclusion-exclusion principle to count elements in union of sets.
ladders.explanation: The inclusion-exclusion principle is a combinatorial technique to count the number of elements in the union of multiple sets. For sets A1, A2, ..., Ak, the principle states: |A1 ∪ A2 ∪ ... ∪ Ak| = Σ|Ai| - Σ|Ai ∩ Aj| + Σ|Ai ∩ Aj ∩ Ak| - ... + (-1)^(k+1)|A1 ∩ A2 ∩ ... ∩ Ak|. In this problem, each set Ai represents numbers divisible by prime pi. The intersection of sets corresponds to numbers divisible by the LCM of those primes. The 'at least one' condition translates to the union of sets. The alternating signs ensure each element is counted exactly once: elements in exactly one set are counted once in the first sum; elements in two sets are counted twice in first sum but subtracted once in the second sum, net count 1; and so on. The intuition is to overcount by adding individual counts, then correct overlaps by subtracting pairwise intersections, but this over-corrects for higher-order overlaps, so we add back triple intersections, etc. This works because the primes are distinct, so their LCMs are simply products. Time complexity for applying the formula is O(2^k) if we enumerate all subsets, and space is O(1) for storing the total. The connection to L1 is that this logic is implemented via recursive subset traversal. Common mistakes include incorrect sign application (should be + for odd-sized subsets, - for even-sized), forgetting to exclude the empty subset, and not using LCM for intersections when primes are not pairwise coprime (though they are primes, so LCM = product).

examples.input: Sets: A={multiples of 2 up to 20}, B={multiples of 3 up to 20}
examples.trace: Step 1: |A| = floor(20/2)=10. |B| = floor(20/3)=6. Step 2: |A ∩ B| = multiples of lcm(2,3)=6 up to 20 = floor(20/6)=3. Step 3: Apply inclusion-exclusion: |A ∪ B| = |A| + |B| - |A ∩ B| = 10 + 6 - 3 = 13.

examples.input: Sets: A={multiples of 2 up to 30}, B={multiples of 3 up to 30}, C={multiples of 5 up to 30}
examples.trace: Step 1: |A|=15, |B|=10, |C|=6. Step 2: Pairwise intersections: |A∩B|=floor(30/6)=5, |A∩C|=floor(30/10)=3, |B∩C|=floor(30/15)=2. Step 3: Triple intersection: |A∩B∩C|=floor(30/30)=1. Step 4: Inclusion-exclusion: |A∪B∪C| = (15+10+6) - (5+3+2) + 1 = 31 - 10 + 1 = 22.

testCases.input: |A|=8, |B|=5, |C|=3, |D|=2, all pairwise intersections=1, all triple intersections=0, quadruple intersection=0
|A|=30, |B|=20, |C|=15, |D|=10, all pairwise intersections=5, all triple intersections=2, quadruple intersection=1

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute LCM of Multiple Numbers
ladders.desc: Calculate the Least Common Multiple (LCM) for a list of integers.
ladders.explanation: The Least Common Multiple (LCM) of two numbers a and b is the smallest positive integer divisible by both a and b. It is computed as lcm(a,b) = a * b / gcd(a,b), where gcd is the greatest common divisor. For multiple numbers, lcm(a,b,c) = lcm(lcm(a,b), c), and so on iteratively. In this problem, since the inputs are distinct primes, the LCM is simply the product of the primes in the subset (because gcd of distinct primes is 1). However, the general LCM computation is crucial for understanding the approach when dealing with non-prime numbers or when extending the problem. The intuition is that the intersection of sets of multiples corresponds to multiples of the LCM. For example, numbers divisible by both 2 and 3 are divisible by lcm(2,3)=6. The algorithm: initialize lcm=1, for each number in the list, update lcm = lcm * num // gcd(lcm, num). Time complexity is O(m * log(max(num))) for m numbers, using Euclidean algorithm for gcd. Space is O(1). The connection to L2 is that LCM is used to compute the size of intersections in inclusion-exclusion. Common mistakes include integer overflow when multiplying before dividing (should compute as lcm * num // gcd(lcm, num) to minimize overflow), and assuming LCM is just product for non-coprime numbers. In Python, math.lcm is available from 3.9+, but implementing manually reinforces understanding.

examples.trace: Step 1: lcm=1. Step 2: Process 2: gcd(1,2)=1, lcm=1*2//1=2. Step 3: Process 3: gcd(2,3)=1, lcm=2*3//1=6. Output: 6.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Count Multiples of a Number Up to N
ladders.desc: Compute how many integers from 1 to N are divisible by a given integer d.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation counts the number of multiples of d that are ≤ N. The count is given by floor(N / d), i.e., integer division of N by d. The intuition is that multiples of d are d, 2d, 3d, ..., up to the largest k such that k*d ≤ N. This k is exactly floor(N/d). For example, multiples of 3 up to 10 are 3,6,9 → floor(10/3)=3. This works because the multiples form an arithmetic sequence with difference d, and the count is the number of terms. The algorithm is straightforward: count = N // d in integer arithmetic. Time complexity is O(1), space O(1). The connection to L3 is that this count is used when computing the size of sets in inclusion-exclusion (e.g., |A| = floor(N/2) for multiples of 2). Common mistakes include using floating-point division and rounding incorrectly, or not handling d > N (where count is 0). Also, ensure to use integer division to avoid precision issues. This operation is essential for many number theory problems.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute floor(20/4). Step 2: 20 ÷ 4 = 5 exactly. Step 3: Multiples of 4 up to 20: 4,8,12,16,20 → 5 numbers. Output: 5.
Step 1: Compute floor(15/7). Step 2: 15 ÷ 7 = 2 with remainder 1, so floor is 2. Step 3: Multiples of 7 up to 15: 7,14 → 2 numbers. Output: 2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Integer Division and Floor Function
ladders.desc: Understand integer division (floor division) and its properties.
ladders.explanation: Integer division, often denoted as a // b in programming, returns the largest integer less than or equal to the exact quotient a/b. It is also called floor division because it applies the floor function to the result. For example, 7 // 3 = 2 because 7/3 ≈ 2.333 and floor(2.333)=2. The intuition is that it counts how many times b fits completely into a, ignoring the remainder. Key properties: 1) For positive integers, a // b = floor(a/b). 2) It distributes over addition in a limited way: (a+b)//c is not necessarily equal to a//c + b//c. 3) a // b = 0 if a < b. This operation is fundamental in counting problems, like determining how many multiples of a number exist within a range. In the context of this problem, we use it to count multiples: floor(N/d) gives the number of integers ≤ N divisible by d. Time and space complexity are O(1). The connection to L4 is that this operation is used directly to count multiples. Common mistakes include confusing it with floating-point division or ceiling division, and not handling negative numbers (though in this problem, N and d are positive). Beginners should practice with examples to build intuition.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute exact quotient: 10 ÷ 3 = 3.333... Step 2: Apply floor function: floor(3.333...) = 3. Step 3: Verify: 3 * 3 = 9 ≤ 10, 4 * 3 = 12 > 10, so 3 fits. Output: 3.
Step 1: Compute exact quotient: 5 ÷ 7 ≈ 0.714. Step 2: Apply floor function: floor(0.714) = 0. Step 3: Verify: 0 * 7 = 0 ≤ 5, 1 * 7 = 7 > 5, so 0 fits. Output: 0.

approaches.name: Inclusion-Exclusion with Bitmask O(2^k)
approaches.summary: Use bitmask iteration to generate all subsets of primes, compute LCMs for each subset, and apply inclusion-exclusion formulaically. This is more efficient than recursion for implementation and avoids deep recursion overhead.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count numbers divisible by at least one prime using inclusion-exclusion with bitmask
ladders.desc: Complete solution counting integers ≤ N divisible by at least one given prime using inclusion-exclusion principle with bitmask iteration.
ladders.explanation: The inclusion-exclusion principle solves the problem of counting numbers divisible by at least one of several primes by systematically including and excluding counts of numbers divisible by combinations of primes. For k primes, there are 2^k subsets (excluding the empty set). Each subset corresponds to a combination of primes, and the count of numbers divisible by all primes in that subset is floor(N / LCM(primes_in_subset)). The inclusion-exclusion principle alternates signs: subsets with odd size add to the total, while subsets with even size subtract. This works because numbers divisible by multiple primes get counted multiple times in naive addition, and the alternating signs correct for this overcounting. The bitmask approach iterates through all subsets from 1 to (1<<k)-1, where each bit position corresponds to including a prime. For each mask, we compute the LCM of selected primes (ensuring it doesn't exceed N to avoid overflow), count numbers divisible by this LCM as floor(N/LCM), and add or subtract based on the parity of bits set. The time complexity is O(2^k * k) for LCM computation, but typically O(2^k) as k is small. Space is O(1). This approach is efficient and avoids recursion overhead, making it suitable for k up to about 20. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle LCM overflow (stop if LCM > N), incorrect sign handling (odd subsets add, even subtract), and off-by-one errors in bitmask iteration. The solution connects directly to L1 by using the subset generation and LCM computation as building blocks.

examples.trace:
Step 1: k=2 primes, subsets from 1 to 3 (binary 01,10,11).
Step 2: Mask=01 (binary), primes_in_subset=[2], LCM=2, count=floor(20/2)=10, bits=1 (odd), add 10 → total=10.
Step 3: Mask=10 (binary), primes_in_subset=[3], LCM=3, count=floor(20/3)=6, bits=1 (odd), add 6 → total=16.
Step 4: Mask=11 (binary), primes_in_subset=[2,3], LCM=6, count=floor(20/6)=3, bits=2 (even), subtract 3 → total=13.
Step 5: Result=13 numbers (2,3,4,6,8,9,10,12,14,15,16,18,20).

Step 1: k=3 primes, subsets from 1 to 7.
Step 2: Mask=001: primes=[2], LCM=2, count=15, odd → total=15.
Step 3: Mask=010: primes=[3], LCM=3, count=10, odd → total=25.
Step 4: Mask=100: primes=[5], LCM=5, count=6, odd → total=31.
Step 5: Mask=011: primes=[2,3], LCM=6, count=5, even → total=26.
Step 6: Mask=101: primes=[2,5], LCM=10, count=3, even → total=23.
Step 7: Mask=110: primes=[3,5], LCM=15, count=2, even → total=21.
Step 8: Mask=111: primes=[2,3,5], LCM=30, count=1, odd → total=22.
Step 9: Result=22 numbers.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate all subsets of primes and compute LCM for inclusion-exclusion
ladders.desc: Iterate through all non-empty subsets of primes using bitmask and compute LCM for each subset.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core engine of the inclusion-exclusion approach. It systematically explores all combinations of the given primes to apply the inclusion-exclusion principle. For k primes, there are 2^k - 1 non-empty subsets. A bitmask of length k represents each subset: bit i set to 1 means prime i is included. We iterate masks from 1 to (1<<k)-1. For each mask, we extract the selected primes by checking bits, compute their LCM, and determine the count contribution as floor(N/LCM). The sign is determined by the number of bits set (popcount): odd → add, even → subtract. The intuition is that each subset corresponds to numbers divisible by all primes in that subset, and the alternating sign corrects for overcounting. The LCM computation uses the formula LCM(a,b) = a*b / GCD(a,b), applied iteratively. We must stop if LCM exceeds N, as further contributions are zero. Time complexity is O(2^k * k) for iterating and computing LCMs. Space is O(k) for storing primes, but O(1) extra. This feeds into L0 by providing the mechanism to apply inclusion-exclusion. Common mistakes include not handling LCM overflow (use if LCM > N: break), incorrect bitmask range, and miscomputing LCM. The real code pattern uses a loop over masks and bit checking.

examples.trace:
Step 1: k=2, masks 1 to 3.
Step 2: Mask=1 (01): bits=1, primes=[2], LCM=2, count=floor(12/2)=6, sign=+.
Step 3: Mask=2 (10): bits=1, primes=[3], LCM=3, count=floor(12/3)=4, sign=+.
Step 4: Mask=3 (11): bits=2, primes=[2,3], LCM=6, count=floor(12/6)=2, sign=-.
Step 5: Output subsets and counts.

Step 1: k=2, masks 1 to 3.
Step 2: Mask=1: primes=[2], LCM=2, count=10, sign=+.
Step 3: Mask=2: primes=[5], LCM=5, count=4, sign=+.
Step 4: Mask=3: primes=[2,5], LCM=10, count=2, sign=-.
Step 5: Output.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Inclusion-exclusion principle for counting divisible numbers
ladders.desc: Apply inclusion-exclusion formula to count numbers divisible by at least one of several primes.
ladders.explanation: The inclusion-exclusion principle is a combinatorial technique to count the union of sets by adding sizes of individual sets, subtracting sizes of pairwise intersections, adding sizes of triple intersections, and so on. For primes p1, p2, ..., pk, let A_i be the set of numbers ≤ N divisible by p_i. We want |A_1 ∪ A_2 ∪ ... ∪ A_k|. The formula is: |union| = Σ|A_i| - Σ|A_i ∩ A_j| + Σ|A_i ∩ A_j ∩ A_l| - ... + (-1)^(k+1)|intersection of all|. Each intersection corresponds to numbers divisible by the LCM of the primes in that subset. The sign alternates: + for odd-sized subsets, - for even-sized subsets. Intuitively, when we add all |A_i|, numbers divisible by multiple primes are counted multiple times; subtracting pairwise intersections removes double-counting but over-removes numbers in triple intersections, so we add them back, etc. This correction ensures each number is counted exactly once. The time to compute all terms is exponential in k, but efficient for small k. Space is O(1). This feeds into L1 by providing the mathematical basis for the subset processing. Common mistakes include incorrect sign alternation or missing subsets. The real code pattern sums over subsets with sign = (-1)^(size+1).

examples.trace:
Step 1: A = numbers divisible by 2: {2,4,6,8,10,12}, |A|=6.
Step 2: B = numbers divisible by 3: {3,6,9,12}, |B|=4.
Step 3: A∩B = numbers divisible by both 2 and 3 (LCM=6): {6,12}, |A∩B|=2.
Step 4: Apply formula: 6+4-2=8.
Step 5: Verify: numbers divisible by at least one: {2,3,4,6,8,9,10,12} count=8.

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute LCM of multiple numbers
ladders.desc: Calculate the Least Common Multiple (LCM) of a list of integers iteratively.
ladders.explanation: The Least Common Multiple (LCM) of numbers is the smallest positive integer divisible by all of them. For primes, LCM is simply their product, but in general, for numbers a and b, LCM(a,b) = a*b / GCD(a,b), where GCD is the Greatest Common Divisor. To compute LCM of multiple numbers, we start with LCM=1 and update iteratively: LCM = LCM * num / GCD(LCM, num). This works because LCM is associative. The intuition is that LCM accumulates the minimal factors needed to cover all numbers. We must ensure integer operations and handle overflow by checking if LCM exceeds a limit (e.g., N). Time complexity is O(m * log(max(num))) for m numbers, using Euclidean algorithm for GCD. Space is O(1). This feeds into L2 by providing the value needed for intersection sizes in inclusion-exclusion. Common mistakes include not using integer division, ignoring overflow, or incorrect order of operations. The real code pattern uses a loop and gcd function.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start LCM=1.
Step 2: Process 2: GCD(1,2)=1, LCM=1*2/1=2.
Step 3: Process 3: GCD(2,3)=1, LCM=2*3/1=6.
Step 4: Result 6.

Step 1: LCM=1.
Step 2: Process 4: GCD(1,4)=1, LCM=1*4/1=4.
Step 3: Process 6: GCD(4,6)=2, LCM=4*6/2=24/2=12.
Step 4: Result 12.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute GCD using Euclidean algorithm
ladders.desc: Calculate the Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) of two integers using the Euclidean algorithm.
ladders.explanation: The Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) of two numbers a and b is the largest integer that divides both. The Euclidean algorithm computes GCD efficiently by repeatedly applying: GCD(a,b) = GCD(b, a mod b) until b=0, then GCD=a. This works because the GCD remains unchanged under modulo operations. Intuitively, we reduce the problem size by replacing the larger number with its remainder when divided by the smaller. The algorithm runs in O(log(min(a,b))) time. Space is O(1) for iterative version. This feeds into L3 as GCD is needed for LCM computation. Common mistakes include not handling negative numbers (take absolute values), infinite loops if not updating variables correctly, and recursion depth issues for large numbers. The real code pattern uses a while loop or recursion.

examples.trace:
Step 1: GCD(48,18).
Step 2: 48 mod 18 = 12, so GCD(18,12).
Step 3: 18 mod 12 = 6, so GCD(12,6).
Step 4: 12 mod 6 = 0, so GCD(6,0)=6.
Step 5: Result 6.

Step 1: GCD(17,13).
Step 2: 17 mod 13 = 4, so GCD(13,4).
Step 3: 13 mod 4 = 1, so GCD(4,1).
Step 4: 4 mod 1 = 0, so GCD(1,0)=1.
Step 5: Result 1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Floor division and counting multiples
ladders.desc: Count how many integers ≤ N are divisible by a given integer d using floor division.
ladders.explanation: To count numbers ≤ N that are divisible by d, we compute floor(N/d), i.e., integer division. This works because the multiples of d are d, 2d, 3d, ..., up to the largest k such that k*d ≤ N. The count k is exactly floor(N/d). Intuitively, we are partitioning N into groups of size d. For example, if N=10 and d=3, multiples are 3,6,9, so count=3, which is floor(10/3)=3. This is a fundamental operation in number theory and combinatorics. Time and space are O(1). This feeds into L4 as it's used in GCD/LCM contexts for counting divisible numbers. Common mistakes include using floating-point division or off-by-one errors. The real code pattern uses N//d in Python.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Multiples of 2 ≤ 10: 2,4,6,8,10.
Step 2: Count them: 5.
Step 3: Compute floor(10/2)=5.
Step 4: Result 5.

Step 1: Multiples of 3 ≤ 10: 3,6,9.
Step 2: Count: 3.
Step 3: floor(10/3)=3.
Step 4: Result 3.
